text: There is a small bilateral pleural effusion, greater on the right than the left, with associated compressive atelectasis at the lung bases.
The endotracheal tube is in satisfactory position with the tip approximately 4 cm above the carina. A right internal jugular central venous catheter terminates in the superior vena cava.
The heart is mildly enlarged. There is no pericardial effusion. The mediastinal contours are within normal limits.
New patchy opacity in the right lower lobe is concerning for developing pneumonia. Left lung remains clear.
Status post median sternotomy and coronary artery bypass graft surgery. Sternal wires are intact. A dual-chamber pacemaker is noted with leads in the right atrium and right ventricle.
There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the right lung. A right-sided chest tube has been placed.
Stable appearance of bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest measuring 8 mm in the right upper lobe. No new nodules identified. Recommend follow-up CT in 6 months.
Worsening consolidation in the left lower lobe with air bronchograms, suggestive of lobar pneumonia. Small left pleural effusion has slightly increased compared to prior examination.
No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality. The lungs are well expanded and clear. Heart size is normal. The osseous structures demonstrate mild degenerative changes in the thoracic spine.
A nasogastric tube courses below the diaphragm with the tip projecting over the gastric body. Bilateral basal atelectasis is noted without focal consolidation.
